bytes.topic: Pipeline Design ‚Äî lint ‚Üí test ‚Üí build ‚Üí deploy stages, fail fast principle
bytes.title: Jenkins Pipeline Stages ‚Äî Enforcing Fail Fast with lint ‚Üí test ‚Üí build ‚Üí deploy
bytes.body:
Jenkins Declarative Pipelines enforce fail-fast by default: if any stage exits non-zero, downstream stages are skipped and the build is marked FAILURE. Structure your stages in ascending cost order ‚Äî lint first (cheapest), then test, then build artifacts, then deploy ‚Äî so expensive work never runs on broken code.

Use `failFast: true` inside a `parallel` block to abort sibling branches the moment one fails. For the lint stage, wire in your actual tool (e.g. `sh 'npm run lint -- --max-warnings 0'`) so warnings become hard errors. Gate the deploy stage with a `when { branch 'main' }` condition so feature branches never accidentally push to production.

Stash build artifacts between stages with `stash`/`unstash` rather than rebuilding ‚Äî Jenkins agents don't share a workspace by default. Use `post { always { junit 'reports/**/*.xml' } }` to surface test results even on failure, giving you signal without re-running the pipeline. Keep each stage's `sh` calls focused; fat stages hide the failure point and slow down red-to-fix feedback loops.

bytes.topic: GitHub Actions ‚Äî workflows, jobs, steps, reusable workflows, marketplace actions
bytes.title: Jenkins to GitHub Actions ‚Äî Pipelines, Shared Libraries, and Plugins Remapped
bytes.body: If you live in Jenkinsfile land, GitHub Actions has a 1-to-1 mental model ‚Äî once you know the vocabulary. A workflow (.github/workflows/*.yml) is your Jenkinsfile. Jobs are your stages, but run in isolated VMs in parallel by default ‚Äî no parallel {} block needed. Steps inside a job map directly to your sh or bat steps, plus uses: to pull a marketplace action (think plugins, but versioned via Git tags, not a plugin manager). Shared Libraries become reusable workflows: define a workflow in .github/workflows/build-template.yml with on: workflow_call:, then reference it with uses: org/repo/.github/workflows/build-template.yml@main. Inputs replace your library function parameters. One critical difference from Jenkins: there's no built-in agent inheritance. Each job declares its own runs-on, so you can't set a global agent at the pipeline level. Secrets from Jenkins credentials() become secrets.MY_SECRET, scoped per job or the whole workflow. Matrix builds that previously needed Jenkins plugins are native ‚Äî just drop a matrix: block under strategy:.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI
on: [push]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - run: npm ci && npm test

  deploy:
    needs: build
    uses: my-org/shared-workflows/.github/workflows/deploy.yml@main
    with:
      environment: staging
    secrets: inherit

bytes.topic: GitLab CI ‚Äî stages, artifacts, cache, runners, include templates
bytes.title: Jenkins Dev's Field Guide to GitLab CI: Stages, Artifacts, and Shared Templates
bytes.body: Coming from Jenkins, GitLab CI's `.gitlab-ci.yml` feels familiar but has sharp differences worth knowing upfront. Stages in GitLab CI work like Jenkins `stages {}` blocks ‚Äî jobs in the same stage run in parallel, stages run sequentially. Unlike Jenkins where `stash`/`unstash` moves files between stages, GitLab uses `artifacts: paths` to persist outputs and `dependencies` to pull them into downstream jobs explicitly. Cache is separate ‚Äî it's for reusing slow-to-build directories like `node_modules` between pipelines, keyed by branch and a cache key. Runners replace Jenkins agents: you register a runner with a tag (e.g., `docker`, `k8s`), then target it via `tags:` in each job ‚Äî no `agent { label '' }` syntax. The biggest power unlock is `include:` ‚Äî GitLab's equivalent of Jenkins Shared Libraries. You can compose pipelines from reusable template files hosted in the same repo or a central project, pulling in `.gitlab-ci.yml` fragments with `include: project:` or `include: local:`. This replaces the ceremony of `@Library` declarations and `vars/` scripts entirely.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .gitlab-ci.yml ‚Äî annotated for Jenkins developers
stages: [build, test, deploy]

build-job:
  stage: build
  tags: [docker]          # like: agent { label 'docker' }
  script:
    - npm ci
    - npm run build
  artifacts:
    paths: [dist/]        # like: stash includes: 'dist/**'
    expire_in: 1 hour
  cache:
    key: $CI_COMMIT_REF_SLUG
    paths: [node_modules/]

include:
  - project: 'org/ci-templates'
    file: '/templates/deploy.yml'  # like: @Library('ci-templates')import deploy

bytes.topic: Jenkins ‚Äî pipeline as code, Jenkinsfile, shared libraries, agent config
bytes.title: Jenkins Shared Libraries ‚Äî Reuse Pipeline Logic Across Every Repo
bytes.body:
When you find yourself copy-pasting the same Jenkinsfile stages across dozens of repos, Jenkins Shared Libraries are the fix. Define a library in a Git repo with the standard layout: `vars/` for global call steps, `src/` for Groovy classes, and `resources/` for static files. Register it in Manage Jenkins ‚Üí Configure System ‚Üí Global Pipeline Libraries, then consume it with `@Library('my-lib@main') _` at the top of any Jenkinsfile.

Steps defined in `vars/buildAndPush.groovy` become first-class pipeline steps ‚Äî call them like `buildAndPush(image: 'myapp', tag: env.GIT_COMMIT)`. Use `src/org/myorg/Docker.groovy` for complex logic that benefits from a proper class structure with CPS-safe methods.

Agent config belongs in the library too. Define a `podTemplate` wrapper in `vars/k8sAgent.groovy` so every team gets the same Kubernetes agent spec without duplicating YAML. Pair this with `Jenkinsfile` containing only business logic ‚Äî the library owns the how, the repo owns the what.

bytes.topic: Secrets in CI ‚Äî never hardcode, vault integration, environment secrets
bytes.title: Jenkins Secrets Done Right ‚Äî Credentials Plugin, Vault Integration, and What to Never Do
bytes.body: Hardcoding credentials in a Jenkinsfile is a critical misconfig ‚Äî they end up in SCM history and console logs. Jenkins' built-in Credentials Plugin is your first line of defense. Store secrets as `Secret text`, `Username with password`, or `Secret file` in Manage Jenkins ‚Üí Credentials, then bind them in-pipeline using `withCredentials`. For external secret management, the HashiCorp Vault Plugin lets Jenkins authenticate via AppRole or Kubernetes service accounts and fetch secrets at runtime ‚Äî secrets never touch the Jenkins database. Declare vault bindings with `vault secrets: [...]` in your `withVault` block. For environment-scoped secrets, prefer folder-level or pipeline-level credential scoping over global credentials to enforce least privilege. Always set `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY=false` in production. One often-missed detail: Jenkins masks bound credentials in console output, but only for the exact string ‚Äî base64-encoded variants will still leak. Use `credentials()` in `environment {}` blocks for implicit masking across all steps, not just inside `withCredentials`.

bytes.topic: Caching ‚Äî dependency caching, Docker layer caching, cache invalidation
bytes.title: Jenkins Caching ‚Äî Dependency Caching, Docker Layer Caching, and Cache Invalidation
bytes.body:
Jenkins has no built-in caching layer ‚Äî you implement it explicitly. For Maven/Gradle, mount the local repo as a volume or use the `stash`/`unstash` steps to persist `.m2` or `.gradle` across stages. Better: share a host-level cache directory via `ws()` or a persistent agent volume, so all builds on that node reuse downloaded artifacts without re-fetching from Artifactory.

For Docker builds, enable BuildKit with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1` and ensure your `Dockerfile` orders layers from least- to most-frequently changed ‚Äî base image, system deps, then app code. On the same agent, the layer cache is on-disk automatically. For distributed agents or ephemeral cloud agents, push intermediate layers to a registry using `--cache-from` and `--cache-to` with `registry` mode.

Cache invalidation is the hard part. Tie cache keys to lockfile hashes: compute `sha256sum package-lock.json` and use that as a directory name under your cache root. When the lockfile changes, the path misses and a fresh install runs. Stale caches cause silent failures ‚Äî add a `post { always { cleanWs() } }` on the branch that owns the cache volume, scheduled weekly.

bytes.topic: Parallelization ‚Äî matrix builds, parallel jobs, test sharding
bytes.title: Jenkins Matrix Builds ‚Äî Parallel Jobs and Test Sharding at Scale
bytes.body:
Jenkins' `matrix` directive (Pipeline 2.12+) lets you define a cross-product of axes, spawning isolated agents per combination ‚Äî no scripted loops required. Define axes for OS, JDK version, or test suite partition, and Jenkins schedules each cell concurrently across available executors.

For test sharding, pair matrix with `excludes` to drop redundant combinations, then pass the `SHARD_INDEX` and `SHARD_TOTAL` environment variables into your test runner (e.g., pytest-split, Jest --shard). Each cell runs only its slice, then `junit` merges reports post-build.

Use `parallel` blocks inside a stage for ad-hoc fan-out without the combinatorial overhead ‚Äî ideal when you need three fixed test buckets, not a full matrix. Cap concurrency with `options { throttleJobProperty(...) }` via the Throttle Concurrent Builds plugin to avoid starving other pipelines on shared agents.

Critical: set `failFast true` inside `matrix` so a failing cell kills siblings immediately, preventing wasted executor time on a build you'll re-run anyway.

bytes.topic: Build Artifacts ‚Äî versioning, storing, promoting across environments
bytes.title: Jenkins Build Artifacts ‚Äî Versioning, Storing, and Promoting Across Environments
bytes.body:
Jenkins treats artifacts as first-class citizens via `archiveArtifacts` and the `copyArtifacts` plugin. Version your builds deterministically using `BUILD_NUMBER` combined with a Git short SHA: `${BUILD_NUMBER}-${GIT_COMMIT[0..7]}`. Archive with `archiveArtifacts artifacts: 'build/libs/*.jar', fingerprint: true` ‚Äî fingerprinting enables `fingerprintArtifacts` tracking across downstream jobs.

For promotion, define a pipeline per environment (dev ‚Üí staging ‚Üí prod). Downstream jobs pull artifacts using `copyArtifacts(projectName: 'my-app', selector: lastSuccessful(), filter: '**/*.jar')`. Combine this with the `Promoted Builds` plugin to gate promotions behind manual approval steps using `input message: 'Promote to production?'`.

Store artifacts durably by configuring an S3-backed artifact manager (`ArtifactManagerConfiguration` ‚Üí AWS S3) instead of the default workspace storage, which evicts on executor cleanup. Use `BuildDiscarder(logRotator(artifactNumToKeepStr: '10'))` to cap retention. Tag promoted artifacts in Artifactory or Nexus via a `curl` post-build step, embedding the Jenkins `BUILD_URL` as provenance metadata.

bytes.topic: Environment Promotion ‚Äî dev ‚Üí staging ‚Üí prod gates, approval workflows
bytes.title: Jenkins Environment Promotion ‚Äî Gating Dev ‚Üí Staging ‚Üí Prod with input() and Milestone Steps
bytes.body:
Jenkins Declarative Pipelines handle environment promotion via the `input` step, which pauses execution and blocks the executor thread until a user approves or aborts. Pair it with `milestone` steps to cancel any older builds that try to leapfrog a newer one ‚Äî critical for preventing out-of-order deploys when two PRs land close together.

Define each environment as a discrete `stage` with a `when` condition tied to branch name or a promotion parameter. Gate staging behind a `timeout`-wrapped `input` so abandoned pipelines don't block executors indefinitely. For prod, restrict approvers using the `submitter` parameter, which accepts a comma-separated list of Jenkins usernames or group names (e.g., `submitter: 'ops-team,release-manager'`).

Use `lock` from the Lockable Resources plugin on the prod deploy stage to serialize concurrent release trains. Combine with `disableConcurrentBuilds()` in the `options` block for staging to avoid environment drift during parallel runs. Store environment-specific credentials in Jenkins Credentials Store and inject via `withCredentials`, never hardcoded in the `environment` block.

bytes.topic: Rollback Strategy ‚Äî automated rollback on failed deploy
bytes.title: Jenkins Pipelines ‚Äî Automated Rollback on Failed Deploy Using previousSuccessfulBuild
bytes.body:
Jenkins' Declarative Pipeline `post { failure { } }` block is the natural hook for automated rollback. Instead of hardcoding a version, use `currentBuild.previousSuccessfulBuild?.number` ‚Äî a Groovy property on Jenkins' `RunWrapper` object ‚Äî to dynamically target the last known-good artifact.

The pattern pairs cleanly with an image-tag-as-build-number convention: on failure, re-issue the `kubectl set image` command pointing at the previous build's tag. For non-Kubernetes targets, the **Copy Artifact** plugin's `lastSuccessful()` selector does the same job for WAR/JAR artifacts.

Jenkins-specific gotchas to handle:
- `previousSuccessfulBuild` is `null` on the first ever pipeline run ‚Äî always guard with the safe-navigation operator `?.` or the rollback step itself will throw.
- `post { failure }` fires once after the whole pipeline fails, not per-stage ‚Äî for stage-level rollback, wrap each `stage` in a `catchError` block instead.
- Annotate the build with `currentBuild.description = "ROLLED BACK to #${lastGood}"` so the Jenkins UI and audit logs make the rollback visible without digging through console output.

bytes.topic: Preview Environments ‚Äî ephemeral environments per PR, teardown on merge
bytes.title: Jenkins Preview Environments ‚Äî Ephemeral PRs with Automatic Teardown
bytes.body:
Jenkins Multibranch Pipelines expose `env.CHANGE_ID` for every pull request branch, making per-PR preview environments straightforward. Use a `when { changeRequest() }` guard to restrict deploy logic to PRs only, then namespace your environment with `preview-${env.CHANGE_ID}` so multiple PRs never collide.

Deploy in the `Build` or `Deploy` stage using that namespace ‚Äî whether you're running `kubectl apply -n preview-${env.CHANGE_ID}`, spinning up a Docker Compose stack, or provisioning a Kubernetes namespace via Helm with `--namespace pr-${env.CHANGE_ID}`.

Teardown belongs in a `post { cleanup }` block on the PR branch, not the `always` block ‚Äî `cleanup` runs regardless of build outcome but only after all other post conditions. When the PR merges, the Multibranch plugin deletes the branch and triggers a final run of the Jenkinsfile with `currentBuild.result` available, letting you conditionally skip teardown on failure. Pair with the GitHub Branch Source plugin's `Discard old builds` and `Prune stale branches` settings so orphaned environments don't persist if Jenkins misses a merge event.

bytes.topic: Security Scanning ‚Äî SAST, dependency scanning, container scanning in pipeline
bytes.title: Jenkins Security Scanning ‚Äî SAST, Dependency, and Container Checks in One Pipeline
bytes.body: Jenkins pipelines can integrate three distinct scanning layers using purpose-built plugins and CLI tools. For SAST, the Warnings Next Generation plugin (`recordIssues`) ingests reports from tools like Semgrep or SpotBugs ‚Äî run them as a `sh` step and point `recordIssues` at the output file. Dependency scanning fits naturally in a `stage('SCA')` block using OWASP Dependency-Check plugin (`dependencyCheck` / `dependencyCheckPublisher`) ‚Äî it downloads the NVD feed on first run and caches it in `$JENKINS_HOME`. Container scanning belongs after the `docker build` step; invoke Trivy via `sh 'trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL myapp:${BUILD_NUMBER}'`. The `--exit-code 1` flag fails the build on policy violations. Gate the pipeline by wrapping all three stages in a `parallel` block to cut wall-clock time, then aggregate results in a downstream `post { always { ... } }` block. Store reports as artifacts with `archiveArtifacts` so the Jenkins UI surfaces findings without external dashboards.

bytes.topic: GitOps ‚Äî declarative deployments, git as single source of truth
bytes.title: Jenkins GitOps ‚Äî Git as the Single Source of Truth for Kubernetes Deployments
bytes.body:
GitOps flips the traditional Jenkins deploy model: instead of pushing artifacts and running imperative kubectl commands, your Kubernetes manifests live in git and Jenkins reconciles cluster state to match. The Jenkinsfile itself is the IaC ‚Äî checked in, reviewed via PRs, fully auditable.

The `changeset` condition gates the deploy stage so only commits touching `k8s/**` trigger a reconciliation. `kubectl apply --server-side` uses Server-Side Apply, which tracks field ownership and surfaces conflicts that client-side apply silently ignores. Adding `--prune -l app=myservice` enforces the declarative contract: resources deleted from git are deleted from the cluster. No manual cleanup, no drift.

For webhook-driven GitOps (preferred over polling), configure a GitHub webhook to POST to `<jenkins-url>/github-webhook/` and replace `pollSCM` with an empty `triggers {}` block ‚Äî Jenkins reacts instantly to push events instead of lagging up to 2 minutes.

Store your kubeconfig in Jenkins Credentials as a secret file, never a plain-text string, and bind it with `withCredentials([file(...)])` so it is masked in console output and wiped from disk after the step completes.

bytes.topic: Self-hosted Runners ‚Äî when to use, maintenance overhead, security
bytes.title: Jenkins Self-Hosted Agents ‚Äî When to Use Them and What They Cost You
bytes.body:
Jenkins' built-in controller can run builds directly, but `agent { label 'linux' }` points to a dedicated node registered via JNLP or SSH. Self-hosted agents make sense when builds need specific hardware (GPU, ARM), proprietary tools, or access to air-gapped internal services that Jenkins SaaS agents can't reach.

The hidden cost is maintenance: every agent node requires OS patching, JDK version alignment with the controller, and plugin-compatible remoting JARs. Drift between nodes causes `NoSuchMethodError` at runtime ‚Äî pin `remoting.jar` versions via the `jenkins.remoting.version` system property and lock JDK versions with a shared Ansible role or Docker image.

For security, agents run as a dedicated `jenkins` OS user with no sudo. Enable `Agent ‚Üí Controller Security` in `Manage Jenkins ‚Ä∫ Security` to prevent agent code from reading controller secrets. Use credential binding via the `withCredentials` step rather than environment variables in `Jenkinsfile`, so secrets never appear in `env` dumps. Isolate untrusted PR builds to ephemeral Docker agents (`docker.image('node:22').inside`) to prevent workspace poisoning across jobs.

bytes.topic: Pipeline Performance ‚Äî measuring and optimizing slow pipelines
bytes.title: Jenkins Pipeline Performance ‚Äî Measuring and Eliminating Bottlenecks
bytes.body:
Jenkins exposes per-stage timing natively via the Pipeline Stage View and Blue Ocean, but raw numbers hide the real culprits. Use the `currentBuild.duration` global and stage-level timestamps with `System.currentTimeMillis()` to instrument granular timings programmatically. For deeper analysis, enable the `pipeline-graph-analysis` plugin and query the REST API at `/job/<name>/<build>/wfapi/describe` ‚Äî it returns each stage's `durationMillis` and `pauseDurationMillis`, separating execution time from queue wait.

Common bottlenecks: (1) Serial stages that could be `parallel {}` blocks ‚Äî Maven module builds and independent test suites are frequent offenders. (2) Workspace checkout on every agent when SCM content hasn't changed ‚Äî mitigate with `ws()` reuse or `skipDefaultCheckout(true)` plus selective `checkout scm`. (3) Stash/unstash of large artifacts ‚Äî prefer a shared NFS mount or S3 via the Artifact Manager S3 plugin. (4) Excessive `sh` subprocess spawning inside loops ‚Äî batch into a single shell heredoc. Profile first with `wfapi`, then parallelize or cache.

bytes.topic: Monorepo CI ‚Äî affected detection, selective runs, turborepo/nx integration
bytes.title: Jenkins Monorepo CI ‚Äî Affected Detection with Turborepo and Selective Stage Runs
bytes.body:
Running your full Jenkins pipeline on every commit in a monorepo burns agent minutes and developer patience. The fix: compute the affected package set first, then conditionally execute downstream stages.

In a `Jenkinsfile`, use `sh 'npx turbo run build --filter=...[HEAD^1]'` to let Turborepo's affected graph determine what changed since the previous commit. For Nx, swap in `npx nx affected --target=build --base=HEAD~1`. Capture the output, parse it, and set pipeline-level environment variables to gate stages.

Pair this with Jenkins' `when { expression }` directive on each stage. A `lint` stage only runs if `env.AFFECTED_LINT == 'true'`, a `deploy` stage only fires if the `api` package is in the affected set. No custom shell scripts sprawling across the repo ‚Äî the logic lives in the `Jenkinsfile` and is auditable.

For caching, mount a persistent Jenkins agent workspace volume and set `TURBO_CACHE_DIR` or `NX_CACHE_DIRECTORY` to a path that survives between builds. This gives you local task-level caching without a remote cache server.

bytes.topic: Notifications ‚Äî failure alerts, status badges, incident escalation
bytes.title: Jenkins Notifications ‚Äî Failure Alerts, Status Badges, and Incident Escalation
bytes.body: Jenkins' built-in post block in Declarative Pipelines gives you granular notification hooks: always, failure, unstable, fixed, and regression. Pair this with the Email Extension Plugin (emailext) or Slack Notification Plugin to fire targeted alerts. For status badges on your repo, the Embeddable Build Status Plugin exposes a URL like /buildStatus/icon?job=my-pipeline&build=last that returns a real-time SVG ‚Äî embed it directly in your README. For incident escalation, chain notification steps using currentBuild.previousBuild.result: if the previous build also failed, escalate to PagerDuty via an HTTP POST using the httpRequest step rather than a simple Slack ping. The Jenkins Monitoring Plugin (JavaMelody) and the Prometheus Metrics Plugin expose /prometheus for scraping ‚Äî wire build failure rates into Grafana alerts for team-wide SLO visibility. Always include BUILD_URL, JOB_NAME, and BUILD_NUMBER in notification payloads so on-call engineers can jump directly to the console log without hunting through the UI.
